outline: Day 1: lower-body machines, 2 to 3 controlled sets each, plus 10 to 15 minutes easy cardio
Day 2: upper-body machines, 2 to 3 controlled sets each, plus 10 to 15 minutes easy cardio
Day 3: 25 to 35 minutes steady cardio with one or two simple accessories
why: Simple plans should stay repeatable and avoid overwhelming the user with too many moving parts.
caution: Treat this as a starting structure; increase load, sets, or cardio minutes gradually rather than changing everything at once.

question_patterns: give me a simple day of meals
outline: Breakfast: a repeatable higher-fiber option with protein support
Lunch: a whole grain or millet base with legumes, dal, or another protein-supportive side
Dinner: a practical lighter evening meal with vegetables and enough volume to feel complete
Snack: one simple filling option if needed, such as fruit with yogurt or a moderate nuts portion
why: Day plans should give a usable shape rather than pretending there is one perfect menu.
caution: Use this as a template and swap meals according to the user's goal, appetite, and available recipes.

outline: Day 1: lower body machines with leg press, leg curl, and leg extension
Day 2: upper body machines with chest press, seated row, and shoulder press
Day 3: steady cardio for 25 to 35 minutes plus core
Day 4: full-body repeat with lighter loads and clean technique
why: Four-day plans should split the week without making every session intense.
caution: Keep at least one easier day so the week stays repeatable.

outline: Session 1: full-body machines, 2 sets each, then easy cardio
Session 2: steady cardio and simple core work
Session 3: full-body machines again, using the same movements to practice setup
Optional Session 4: light walk, bike, or cross trainer only
why: Beginners usually improve faster when they repeat simple movements instead of changing everything daily.
caution: Start with loads that leave clean reps in reserve and increase only after the setup feels consistent.

outline: Two full-body strength days using machines and controlled sets
Two moderate cardio days using treadmill, bike, cross trainer, or walking
One optional light movement day if recovery feels good
Keep sleep, recovery, and consistency as part of the plan
why: PCOS-focused training should combine strength, aerobic work, and recovery rather than chasing extreme intensity.
caution: Progress gradually and keep the plan repeatable across weeks.

outline: Aim for several moderate cardio sessions across the week rather than one hard session
Use treadmill, bike, cross trainer, or stair climber at a repeatable pace
Add two simple strength sessions with leg press, chest press, seated row, and shoulder press
Keep long inactive gaps as low as practical
why: Triglyceride-focused exercise works best as a regular weekly habit pattern.
caution: Chest pain, fainting, severe dizziness, or medically complex cases need medical guidance before exercise planning.

question_patterns: what should i eat today for pcos
outline: Breakfast: higher-fiber breakfast with protein support, such as dal, oats, ragi, or millet-based options
Lunch: vegetables plus legumes or dal with a whole grain or millet base
Dinner: lighter balanced meal that still includes fiber and enough volume
Snack: fruit with yogurt or another filling minimally processed option if needed
why: PCOS day plans should focus on repeatable meal patterns, not a perfect diet.
caution: Use this as a practical template and adjust around appetite, symptoms, and clinician advice.

question_patterns: what should i eat today for triglycerides
outline: Breakfast: savory higher-fiber option instead of a sugary breakfast
Lunch: vegetables, legumes or dal, and a whole grain or millet base
Dinner: lighter meal with vegetables and protein support
Snack: simple filling option without turning it into a dessert-style snack
why: Triglyceride-focused day plans should reduce added sugar and refined carbohydrates while keeping meals realistic.
caution: Alcohol and medication questions need direct clinician guidance when triglycerides are the concern.

outline: Breakfast: soft breakfast such as idli, kanji, oats, or millet upma
Lunch: rasam, dal, kootu, or a vegetable-led bowl with a moderate grain base
Dinner: soup, stew, kichadi, or another lighter complete meal
Snack: fruit with yogurt or a small filling snack only if needed
why: Light day plans should feel gentle without becoming too minimal.
caution: A light day should still include enough fluid, fiber, and protein support to be useful.

outline: Breakfast: dal, pesarattu, oats, ragi, or another breakfast with stronger protein support
Lunch: legume, dal, or bean-based meal with vegetables and a whole grain or millet
Dinner: lighter protein-supportive meal instead of only a plain grain dish
Snack: yogurt, fruit with a protein side, or a moderate nuts portion if needed
why: Higher-protein day plans should distribute protein support across meals instead of relying on one meal.
caution: Keep the day balanced with fiber-rich carbohydrates and vegetables, not only protein numbers.
